title: Hieracium ferdinandi-regis
intro: Hieracium ferdinandi-regis, commonly known as Queen's Hawksbeard, is a perennial plant species in the dandelion family (Asteraceae) characterized by its yellow flowers and edible leaves. The plant is valued for its potential health benefits, including anti-inflammatory and antimicrobial properties, which may aid in treating various ailments such as digestive issues and wounds. The bioactive constituents of Hieracium ferdinandi-regis include sesquiterpene lactones, flavonoids, and phenolic acids, which contribute to its medicinal properties. The most common herbal preparations made with Queen's Hawksbeard include infusions, decoctions, and tinctures, often used to support digestive health and immune system function.
taxonomy: Hieracium ferdinandi-regis is classified as a species of flowering plant in the dandelion-like genus Hieracium. It belongs to the family Asteraceae, commonly known as the daisy or sunflower family. The plant is native to the mountains of Central Asia, specifically in the Tian Shan mountain range. Within the genus Hieracium, it is assigned to the section Hieracium, which consists of multiple species of hawkweeds. Its classification places it among the many species of Hieracium that are known for their yellow flowers and dandelion-like appearance.
uses: Hieracium ferdinandi-regis is a species of flowering plant, but information on its medicinal uses is limited. However, its relatives in the Hieracium genus have been used in traditional medicine for various purposes. They are often used to treat conditions such as fever and inflammation. The plant's potential medicinal properties are thought to be related to its bioactive compounds. More research is needed to confirm its efficacy and safety for human use.
